obit_text:
James Mark Williams was born in Montgomery, Ala., April 12, 1873. He entered the Military Academy in 1890 and graduated number five in his class, June 12, 1894. On the same date he was promoted to be Second Lieutenant, 1st Artillery.

He became First Lieutenant, 7th Artillery, March 2, 1899; Captain, Artillery Corps, July 1, 1901; Major, Coast Artillery Corps, Feb. 2, 1911; Lieut.-Colonel, Coast Artillery Corps, July 1, 1916; and Colonel, Coast Artillery Corps, Oct. 29, 1919. On June 29, 1920, he was retired as Colonel, U. S. A., at his own request and after thirty years' service.

After graduation he spent two years at David's Island, N. Y., and from 1896 to 1900 served as Instructor in the Department of Modern Languages at the Military Academy. During the next three years he was on Coast Artillery duty at Fort Williams, Maine, followed by two years of similar work at the Coast Defense Posts in and around Boston. In 1905 he went to Washington, D. C., for a year of duty as Assistant to the Chief of Artillery, War Department. From 1906 to 1908 he was on duty at Fort Totten, during which time he graduated from the School of Submarine Defense. For the next three years he served with the Coast Artillery at Savannah, Ga., and then for a year on similar duty at Fort Hamilton, N. Y., and Governor's Island, N. Y.

From 1912 to 1915 he was at Fort Monroe, Va., as Editor of the Journal of the United States Artillery and Librarian, Coast Artillery School. Going to the Philippines in 1915, he served for three years with the Coast Defenses of Manila and Subic Bays. Upon his return to the United States he commanded the Coast Defenses of Los Angeles, Calif., for a year and then, in 1919, went to France where he commanded Base Section No. 1, S. O. S., A. E. F., Montoir-en-Bretagne, for a brief period. Returning to the United States late in 1919, he served at Fort Totten, N. Y., until his retirement.

After leaving active service Col. Williams resided at Montgomery, Ala. While motoring from his home to visit his daughter at Fort Totten, N. Y., his motor was struck by a railroad train at Durham, N. C., Aug. 24, 1922, and he was instantly killed together with his wife, Mrs. Madelon Nelson Williams, and his sister, Miss Annie M. Williams.
author: Secretary, Association of Graduates

obit_text:
Edgar B. Robertson was appointed to the U. S. Military Academy from Mass. in 1870 and graduated and promoted to be Second Lieutenant 9th Infantry, June 17, 1874.

He was promoted to First Lieutenant, 9th Infantry, June 15, 1881; to Captain, 9th Infantry, June 15, 1891; to Major of Infantry, 15th Infantry, Jan. 12, 1900; to Lieut.-Colonel, 9th Infantry July 31, 1903; and to Colonel, 9th Infantry, October 6, 1906. He retired from Active Service, October 25, 1907, for Disability in Line of Duty.

For twelve years after his graduation he served on frontier duty at various stations in the Northwest, taking part in the Big Horn Expedition, May 22 to October 27, 1876, and being engaged with Sioux Indians in the fight on Tongue River, Mont., the Battle on Rosebud River, Mont., and the Action at Slim Buttes, Dak. He then served in New Mexico and California for six years followed by a like length of service at Madison Barracks, N. Y.

In 1898 he went to Cuba with his regiment and took part with the 5th Army Corps in the Campaign against Santiago. After his death he was posthumously cited for gallantry in action at Santiago. He was made Major and Quartermaster, U. S. Volunteers, June 3, 1898, and after a short period of service in the office of the Chief Quartermaster, Havana, Cuba, he served as Constructing Quartermaster for a year at Bismark, N. D.

In 1900 he went to China and served there for two years, being for a time Provost Marshall, American Section, Pekin, and also for a time commanding the U. S. Legation Guard at Pekin. Returning to the United States by way of the Philippines, 1902, he served for three years at Fort Niagara, N. Y., and Madison Bks., N. Y., and then went back to the Philippines where he served two years until his retirement.

After leaving active service he made his home in San Francisco where he died, Aug. 1, 1924, at Letterman General Hospital, being survived by his widow and two daughters.
author: Secretary, Association of Graduates

obit_text:
Major Benedict received his flying training in the early days of the Air Corps and served with that branch overseas during the Great War, only to meet death in his plane at Langley Field, Virginia, May 7th, 1925, while engaged in the performance of a tactical mission.

Major Benedict was born in Hastings, Nebraska, February 12, 1890, and entered the United States Military Academy in 1911. While at West Point he was an outstanding figure in athletics, especially football, and maintained a keen interest in sports to the time of his death. The story is told that during one of the Navy games in which he played (he had the distinction of helping to defeat the Navy three times) the ball was about to be put into play after a scrimmage, when it was noticed that Cadet Benedict had been left unconscious on the field.

Shortly after graduation in 1915, he transferred to the Aviation Section, Signal Corps, as the Air Corps was originally called, finishing his flying training at San Diego in the spring of 1917 and going overseas the following January.

His first command in France was the Cadet Detachment at Saint Maixent. At that time the Aviation Section, Signal Corps, was undergoing a tremendous expansion. Cadets in all stages of flying training, some not trained at all, were pouring into France and being assembled at Saint Maixent for assignment to various stations. It was the ability he displayed in the difficult task of handing these young men, so lately come from civil life to a foreign land, to war and the hazards and disappointments of training, that resulted in his being ordered to the Third Aviation Instruction Center at Issoudum, where similar conditions prevailed. During his station at this, the largest pilot school in the A. E. F., he was first in command of the Headquarters Detachment and later in charge of flying.

In August, 1918, when the A. E. F. was preparing for the Argonne offensive, Major Benedict received orders for the Zone of Advance. He had hoped to have command of a squadron at the front, but was sent instead to the Second Corps Aero School at Chatillon-sur-Seine, as Director, and, in November, promoted to the grade of Lieutenant Colonel.

Following the armistice he held successively the posts of commanding officer, Trier Airdrome, Germany; Air Service Commander, Third Corps; and Air Service Commander, Seventh Corps. The latter command took him to Neuwied, Germany, from which station he returned to the United States in August, 1919.

After a few weeks temporary duty in Washington he entered the Air Corps Engineering School, which had just been inaugurated at Dayton, Ohio, taking with him his bride, Miss Dorothy Potter of Chicago. Following graduation a year later he remained on duty with the Engineering Division at McCook (now Wright) Field until February 1922, when he proceeded to London as assistant military attache for aviation.

During his tour at McCook Field he narrowly escaped death while making a practice parachute jump. Owing to the spinning of his body as he leaped from the wing of the plane, the shroud lines of the chute became twisted and it failed to open. Noting that he was falling faster than another officer who had jumped simultaneously from the opposite wing of the plane, he looked up at his chute and was able to open it and the reserve chute he wore in time to avert a calamity.

In August, 1922, orders took him from London to the United States Military Academy for duty in the Department of Tactics. In addition to his duties as L Company's "tac" he lectured on Air Corps subjects and renewed the ever-pleasant memories of his football days by assisting on the coaching staff.

It was two years later that orders came assigning Major Benedict to the Air Corps Tactical School at Langley Field, Virginia, where, in the closing days of the school year, after completing many flying missions in various types of aircraft, he met his death while participating in an aerial attack on a captive balloon, falling on the airdrome in sight of his comrades. Air Corps officers, two of whom were classmates, cadets of L Company and a formation of airplanes from Mitchell Field escorted him to his last station.

Major Benedict was a graduate of the Center of Artillery Studies at Trier, Germany, the Air Corps Engineering School and the Air Corps Tactical School. He leaves behind him, besides his children Charles, Patricia and Calvert, the memory of a powerful personality, a nature clean, straightforward and courageous, without a trace of the pettiness that smudges so many of us. He liked or did not like, and that was all; a thing was right or wrong and that was all. He never quibbled, compromised or regretted. No doubt he would consider his own premature death as properly in line of duty and neither to be wondered at or grieved over. And yet he loved life. Active, athletic, keen, he would go from his work straight to some exercise, preferably out-of-doors, and return home at the day's end refreshed and buoyantly happy, bringing to a family who adored him, the best of himself and of life.
author: D. P. B.
